experiment: Cluster C — atomic vs core-claim verifier x curated vs open-world refs, LIVE answer!=judge
correction: CORRECTS the earlier claim that answer!=judge separation drove clean over-block to 70.6%. A clean re-run shows atomic answer!=judge on CURATED refs over-blocks only 5.9% (not 70.6%); the earlier 70.6% was a STALE RETRIEVE report mis-saved when a prior run hung during a git-stash race. The TRUE over-block driver is OPEN-WORLD retrieval (atomic+retrieve = 52.9% here; earlier retrieve runs 64.7-70.6%), because generic Wikipedia summaries do not entail the specific clean answer.
finding: Core-claim (pass-unless-contradicted) ELIMINATES over-block (->0.0%) in both ref regimes, but it is a fail-OPEN tradeoff: on CURATED refs it keeps contamination-catching high (95.3%, only -2.4pt vs atomic), but on weak OPEN-WORLD refs contamination-catching COLLAPSES to 58.1% (vs atomic 97.7%) because Wikipedia rarely explicitly contradicts the injected fabrication. Atomic is fail-CLOSED (high catch, high open-world over-block); core is fail-OPEN (zero over-block, low open-world catch). Neither dominates — the real lever is REF QUALITY: authoritative independent oracles (Google/Wikidata, per the layered verifier) that actually contradict contamination, not generic retrieval.
